tools.timestamp-guide.seo.keywords: unix timestamp, timestamp guide, seconds vs milliseconds, utc local time, year 2038 problem, timestamp code examples
tools.timestamp-guide.hero.title: Unix Timestamp Complete Guide
tools.timestamp-guide.hero.subtitle: From basic concepts to practical code, fully understand how Unix timestamps work and best practices.
tools.timestamp-guide.whatis.title: What is a Unix Timestamp?
tools.timestamp-guide.whatis.p1: A Unix timestamp (also called Epoch time or POSIX time) represents the number of seconds elapsed since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC (not counting leap seconds). This date is known as the Unix epoch and serves as the universal reference point for time representation in computing.
tools.timestamp-guide.whatis.p2: Because a Unix timestamp is a simple integer, it is unaffected by timezones, daylight saving time, or calendar changes. This makes it the ideal format for exchanging time data across systems and languages. Whether you use Python, JavaScript, Go, or SQL, almost all systems support Unix timestamps.
tools.timestamp-guide.whatis.p3: The only limitation of Unix timestamps is that they can only represent times from 1970 onwards (for 32-bit second-level timestamps, the upper limit is around 2038). For applications that need to represent earlier or later times, millisecond-level timestamps or 64-bit integers are typically used.
tools.timestamp-guide.sec_vs_ms.title: Seconds vs Milliseconds Timestamps
tools.timestamp-guide.sec_vs_ms.subtitle: Comparison of the two most common timestamp formats
tools.timestamp-guide.sec_vs_ms.p1: Second-level timestamps are the original Unix system format, widely used in server-side applications, databases, and system logs. Millisecond-level timestamps are more common in JavaScript (Date.now()), Java, and many modern APIs.
tools.timestamp-guide.sec_vs_ms.p2: Converting between the two is very simple: seconds × 1000 = milliseconds, milliseconds ÷ 1000 = seconds. When processing timestamps, first confirm which format your data source uses, otherwise you may get a 1000× error.
tools.timestamp-guide.utc_vs_local.title: UTC vs Local Time
tools.timestamp-guide.utc_vs_local.p1: UTC (Coordinated Universal Time) is the global time standard and does not change with daylight saving time. A Unix timestamp is essentially a count of UTC time. When you convert a timestamp to 'local time', you are adding your timezone's offset to UTC.
tools.timestamp-guide.utc_vs_local.p2: For example, Beijing time (Asia/Shanghai) has an offset of UTC+8. This means when UTC time is 2024-01-01 00:00:00, Beijing time is 2024-01-01 08:00:00. Both correspond to the same Unix timestamp (1704067200).
tools.timestamp-guide.utc_vs_local.p3: In development, the best practice is: store and transmit using Unix timestamps or UTC time, and only convert to local time when displaying to users. This avoids timezone confusion and daylight saving time issues.
tools.timestamp-guide.code.title: Get Timestamp in Popular Languages
tools.timestamp-guide.code.subtitle: Copy and use, covering mainstream development languages
tools.timestamp-guide.y2038.title: Year 2038 Problem
tools.timestamp-guide.y2038.warning_text: Second-level Unix timestamps stored in 32-bit signed integers will overflow on January 19, 2038 at 03:14:07 UTC, causing time to roll back to 1901.
tools.timestamp-guide.y2038.p1: The Year 2038 problem (Y2K38) is similar to the Y2K bug. System designers at the time believed 32-bit integers were sufficient for timestamps because 2^31-1 seconds is approximately 68 years. Counting from 1970, this point falls exactly in 2038.
tools.timestamp-guide.y2038.p2: Modern systems have widely adopted 64-bit integers for timestamps, extending the time range to hundreds of millions of years. However, some embedded systems, legacy databases, and old code may still use 32-bit timestamps.
tools.timestamp-guide.y2038.p3: If your system needs to run for a long time or store future dates, ensure you use 64-bit timestamps or millisecond format. Most modern programming languages (Python 3, Go, Node.js) default to 64-bit integers, so newly developed applications are usually unaffected.
tools.timestamp-guide.errors.title: Common Errors & Troubleshooting
tools.timestamp-guide.errors.e1_text: When entering 1700000000 (seconds), if treated as milliseconds, you get a date in 1970-01-20. Conversely, entering 1700000000000 (milliseconds) treated as seconds gives 55895-07-01. Always confirm the digit count first: 10 digits = seconds, 13 digits = milliseconds.
tools.timestamp-guide.errors.e2_text: Unix timestamps themselves contain no timezone information. The same timestamp displays differently in Beijing and New York time, but represents the same instant. Don't try to 'convert' the timezone of a timestamp — convert the display format instead.
tools.timestamp-guide.errors.e3_text: Different browsers and languages parse date strings differently. For example, '2024-01-01' may be parsed as UTC in some environments and as local time in others. Use ISO 8601 format (2024-01-01T00:00:00Z) for consistency.
tools.timestamp-guide.errors.e4_text: Unix timestamps do not count leap seconds. This means when a leap second occurs, the timestamp 'skips' a second. For applications requiring sub-second precision (like financial trading), use specialized TAI or GPS time systems.
tools.timestamp-guide.errors.e5_text: Some timezones (like America/New_York) adjust their clocks twice a year. If you calculate time differences around DST transitions, you may see 1-hour discrepancies. Use timezone-name-based libraries (like moment-timezone, date-fns-tz) to handle these issues.

tools.base.bitwidth.note: The same bit pattern can mean different numbers depending on bit width and signedness. Pay attention to this in embedded, networking and protocol work.
tools.base.batch.title: Batch Conversion
tools.base.batch.subtitle: One number per line. The tool detects the base for each line or follows the base you pick.
tools.base.examples.title: Common Examples
tools.base.examples.subtitle: Click any example to load it into the workspace.
tools.base.accuracy.title: About Big Numbers and Precision
tools.base.accuracy.body1: Base conversion should always be done with integers. JavaScript Number values are accurate only up to Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER (2^53 − 1); larger decimal numbers can drift if treated as floats.
tools.base.accuracy.body2: This tool uses native BigInt internally, so it converts arbitrarily large integers without losing precision. Use the BigInt literal format (123n) when copying values back into your JavaScript code.
tools.base.accuracy.body3: Fractional / decimal-point conversion is not supported — only integer parts are converted. Need that? Open an issue.
tools.base.related.title: Related Developer Tools
tools.base.related.base64_desc: Encode binary data as Base64 text.
tools.base.guide_cta.title: Want the deeper story?
tools.base.guide_cta.subtitle: Read the full guide on number bases, prefixes, two's complement and language-specific notation.
tools.base-guide.seo.title: Number Base Conversion Guide — Binary, Octal, Decimal, Hex, Two's Complement | json
tools.base-guide.seo.description: Full reference for number base conversion: binary, octal, decimal, hex, 0b/0o/0x prefixes, two's complement, signed/unsigned values, and code in JS, Python, C, Go and Shell.
tools.base-guide.seo.keywords: number base guide, binary octal decimal hex, two's complement, 0b 0o 0x prefix, signed unsigned integer, javascript python c go base conversion
tools.base-guide.hero.title: Number Base Conversion Guide
tools.base-guide.hero.subtitle: From core concepts to language-specific syntax — everything you need to read and write numbers in any base.
tools.base-guide.whatis.title: What is Base Conversion?
tools.base-guide.whatis.p1: A number's value is independent of how you write it. "255", "0xFF" and "11111111" are different notations for the same quantity. Base conversion is the act of moving between these notations without changing the underlying value.
tools.base-guide.whatis.p2: Computers store everything as binary internally, but humans rarely read raw binary. Hexadecimal compresses four binary digits into one symbol, octal three. Decimal stays familiar but maps poorly to byte boundaries — that is why low-level work leans on hex and binary.
tools.base-guide.whatis.p3: When this guide says "base 2" or "base 16" it means the radix used for positional notation. The same idea generalises: any integer base from 2 to 36 is well-defined as long as you have enough digit symbols.
tools.base-guide.systems.title: Common Number Systems
tools.base-guide.systems.subtitle: The four bases that account for almost all real-world programming work.
tools.base-guide.d2b.title: Decimal → Binary
tools.base-guide.d2b.p1: The classic algorithm: divide by 2 repeatedly, write down each remainder, then read the remainders bottom-up. The remainders form the binary digits from least to most significant.
tools.base-guide.d2b.p2: The same procedure works for any target base — divide by the base instead of 2 and use digit symbols beyond 9 (A–F for hex).
tools.base-guide.b2d.title: Binary → Decimal
tools.base-guide.b2d.p1: Multiply each binary digit by 2 raised to its position (counting from 0 on the right) and add the products. The result is the decimal value.
tools.base-guide.hex.title: Hexadecimal in Practice
tools.base-guide.hex.p1: One hex digit packs exactly four bits, so a single byte fits in two hex characters. That is why hex shows up in everything that touches bytes: file dumps, memory inspectors, color codes (#FF5733), MAC addresses, hashes, RGB-to-HSL math.
tools.base-guide.hex.p2: Hex is case-insensitive: 0xff and 0xFF mean the same value. Most ecosystems prefer uppercase for readability; this tool lets you toggle.
tools.base-guide.hex.p3: When you see something like 0x0A, the leading zero is just padding to keep two-digit byte alignment. The value (10) is identical to 0xA.
tools.base-guide.prefix.title: 0b, 0o and 0x Prefixes
tools.base-guide.prefix.p1: 0b marks a binary literal, 0o an octal literal and 0x a hexadecimal literal. They tell humans and parsers "the digits that follow are not decimal".
tools.base-guide.prefix.p2: Modern languages support all three: JavaScript ES6+, Python 3, Rust, Swift. Some older languages omit one or two — pre-C++14 has no 0b, JSON has no prefixes at all and accepts decimal only.
tools.base-guide.prefix.p3: Be careful with bare-leading-zero octal in C and older Bash: 0755 is octal 493, not decimal 755. The explicit 0o755 form removes that ambiguity.
tools.base-guide.twos.title: Two's Complement
tools.base-guide.twos.info_text: It lets the same hardware adder work for both signed and unsigned numbers. Negation is just "flip all bits and add one".
tools.base-guide.twos.p1: In an N-bit two's-complement representation, the top bit signals the sign. Values from 0 to 2^(N−1)−1 are positive; values from 2^(N−1) to 2^N−1 are interpreted as negative numbers from −2^(N−1) to −1.
tools.base-guide.twos.p2: Whether a bit pattern is "−1" or "255" depends entirely on whether the surrounding code treats it as signed or unsigned. There is no flag inside the bits themselves — interpretation is up to the type system.
tools.base-guide.code.title: Base Conversion in Popular Languages
tools.base-guide.code.subtitle: Snippets you can paste straight into a project.
tools.base-guide.errors.title: Common Mistakes
tools.base-guide.errors.e1_text: In C and old shells 0123 is octal 83, not decimal 123. Always use 0o when you mean octal — and avoid bare leading zeros in numbers that should be decimal.
tools.base-guide.errors.e2_text: Hex itself is case-insensitive but inconsistent casing makes diffs noisy and hashes harder to match. Pick uppercase or lowercase per project and stick with it.
tools.base-guide.errors.e3_text: 0xFF is 255 as uint8_t but −1 as int8_t. Always know which type the bits live in before drawing conclusions.
tools.base-guide.errors.e4_text: Numbers above 2^53 lose precision in JavaScript Number. Use BigInt or strings for large IDs and crypto values; never round-trip through parseFloat.
tools.base-guide.errors.e5_text: Bitwise operations in JavaScript silently truncate to 32 bits. Use BigInt bitwise operators (&, |, ^) for wider values.
tools.base-guide.cta.title: Ready to convert?
tools.base-guide.cta.subtitle: Open the converter to try every example from this guide on your own numbers.

tools.regex.seo.article:
What is a Regular Expression (Regex)?

A regular expression, commonly known as regex, is a powerful sequence of characters that defines a search pattern for text matching, extraction, and replacement. Originally developed in the 1950s for theoretical computer science, regex has become an essential tool in every programmer's toolkit for pattern matching and text processing tasks.

How Regex Works

Regex patterns use special characters called metacharacters to define flexible matching rules. For example, \d matches any digit, \w matches word characters, and . matches any character. Combining these with quantifiers like * (zero or more), + (one or more), and ? (optional) allows you to build complex patterns that can validate emails, extract URLs, or parse structured data.

Common Use Cases

  • Form Validation: Validate email addresses, phone numbers, postal codes, and custom input formats
  • Data Extraction: Extract specific patterns like URLs, dates, or prices from large text documents
  • Search & Replace: Perform complex find-and-replace operations across multiple files
  • Log Analysis: Parse server logs to extract error patterns, IP addresses, or timestamps
  • Web Scraping: Extract structured data from HTML content

Regex Syntax Quick Reference

Basic metacharacters include: ^ (start of line), $ (end of line), [] (character class), () (capturing group), and | (alternation). Modern regex engines also support advanced features like lookaheads (?=...) and lookbehinds (?<=...) for complex matching scenarios.

Why Use Our Online Regex Tester?

Our regex tester runs entirely in your browser, meaning your sensitive data never leaves your computer. Features include real-time match highlighting, syntax error detection, pattern explanation, and a built-in cheat sheet. Whether you're debugging a complex pattern or learning regex for the first time, this tool provides instant feedback to help you write accurate expressions.

Privacy & Security

All regex testing happens locally in your browser using JavaScript's native RegExp engine. No data is sent to any server, making this tool safe for testing patterns against sensitive information like passwords, API keys, or personal data.
tools.markdown.seo.article:
What is Markdown?

Markdown is a lightweight markup language created by John Gruber in 2004 that allows you to write formatted text using plain text syntax. It's designed to be easy-to-read and easy-to-write, converting to valid HTML for web publishing. Markdown has become the de facto standard for documentation, README files, forum posts, and content management systems worldwide.

Why Use Markdown?

Markdown separates content from presentation, letting you focus on writing without distraction. Unlike WYSIWYG editors that can produce inconsistent HTML, Markdown generates clean, semantic markup. It's version-control friendly, works in any text editor, and renders consistently across platforms.

Common Markdown Syntax

  • Headers: Use # for H1, ## for H2, etc.
  • Emphasis: *italic* or _italic_, **bold** or __bold__
  • Links: [text](url) creates clickable links
  • Images: ![alt text](image-url)
  • Code: Use backticks for inline code or triple backticks for code blocks
  • Lists: Use - or * for unordered lists, numbers for ordered lists

GitHub Flavored Markdown (GFM)

Our editor supports GitHub Flavored Markdown, an extended version that adds tables, task lists, strikethrough text, and syntax-highlighted code blocks. These extensions make Markdown suitable for technical documentation and software development workflows.

Use Cases

Markdown is perfect for writing README files, technical documentation, blog posts, Slack messages, Jupyter notebooks, static site generators (like Jekyll, Hugo, Hexo), and content management systems. Many developers use it for note-taking with tools like Obsidian and Notion.

Features of This Editor

Our Markdown editor provides a split-pane view with synchronized scrolling, real-time preview, HTML export, and support for tables, code highlighting, and all GFM features. Everything runs locally in your browser—your documents are never uploaded to any server.
tools.timestamp.seo.article:
What is a Unix Timestamp?

A Unix timestamp (also called epoch time or POSIX time) represents the number of seconds that have elapsed since January 1, 1970, 00:00:00 UTC—not counting leap seconds. This date is known as the Unix epoch and serves as a universal reference point for time representation in computing systems.

Why Unix Timestamps Matter

Unix timestamps provide a simple, unambiguous way to represent points in time across different systems and timezones. Because they're stored as single integers, they're easy to compare, calculate differences, and store in databases. Most programming languages, databases, and APIs use Unix timestamps internally for date/time operations.

Common Formats

  • Seconds: The standard Unix timestamp (e.g., 1709251200)
  • Milliseconds: Used by JavaScript and many APIs (e.g., 1709251200000)
  • Microseconds: Used in some high-precision applications

Converting Timestamps

Converting between Unix timestamps and human-readable dates is essential for debugging logs, working with APIs, and database queries. Our tool handles both directions: enter a timestamp to see the corresponding date/time in any timezone, or enter a date to get its timestamp value.

Timezone Handling

While Unix timestamps are always UTC-based, displaying them in local time requires timezone conversion. Our converter supports all major timezones and automatically detects your local timezone. You can also specify custom timezones for precise conversions.

Practical Applications

  • API Development: Many APIs use timestamps for rate limiting and caching
  • Database Queries: Filter records by date ranges using timestamp comparisons
  • Log Analysis: Convert log timestamps for debugging and monitoring
  • Cron Jobs: Schedule tasks using specific timestamp values

Current Time

Our tool displays the current Unix timestamp in real-time, updated every second. This is useful for quick reference when working with time-sensitive operations or debugging time-based logic in your applications.
tools.base.seo.article:
Understanding Number Systems

Number systems are methods of representing numbers using different bases. While humans typically use decimal (base-10) in daily life, computers fundamentally operate in binary (base-2). Understanding different number systems is essential for programming, networking, and low-level computing tasks.

Common Number Systems

  • Binary (Base-2): Uses only 0 and 1. The fundamental language of computers. Each digit represents a bit.
  • Octal (Base-8): Uses digits 0-7. Historically important in computing, still used in Unix file permissions.
  • Decimal (Base-10): The standard system humans use, with digits 0-9.
  • Hexadecimal (Base-16): Uses 0-9 and A-F. Widely used for memory addresses, color codes, and byte representation.

Why Convert Between Bases?

Programmers frequently need to convert between number systems. Hexadecimal is used for color codes in CSS (#FF5733), memory addresses in debugging, and representing byte values. Binary is essential for understanding bitwise operations, flags, and low-level protocols. Octal appears in Unix file permission notation (chmod 755).

How Conversion Works

Each position in a number represents a power of the base. In decimal, the number 123 equals 1×10² + 2×10¹ + 3×10⁰. Similarly, binary 101 equals 1×2² + 0×2¹ + 1×2⁰ = 5 in decimal. Our converter handles these calculations instantly for any supported base.

Practical Applications

  • Web Development: Convert hex color codes to RGB values
  • Networking: Understand IP addresses and subnet masks
  • Systems Programming: Work with memory addresses and bitwise operations
  • Embedded Systems: Configure registers and flags using binary or hex notation

Bitwise Operations

Our tool includes bitwise AND, OR, XOR, and NOT operations, which are fundamental for working with flags, masks, and low-level data manipulation. These operations work directly on the binary representation of numbers.
tools.case.seo.article:
Text Case Conventions Explained

Programming languages and frameworks use different naming conventions for variables, functions, classes, and files. Converting between these conventions is a common task when working across multiple codebases, following style guides, or integrating with APIs that use different conventions.

Common Naming Conventions

  • camelCase: First word lowercase, subsequent words capitalized. Used in JavaScript, Java, Swift. Example: getUserById
  • PascalCase: Every word capitalized. Used for class names in many languages. Example: UserService
  • snake_case: Words separated by underscores, all lowercase. Used in Python, Ruby, database column names. Example: user_email
  • kebab-case: Words separated by hyphens, all lowercase. Used in URLs, CSS classes, file names. Example: user-profile
  • SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE: Like snake_case but uppercase. Used for constants. Example: MAX_RETRY_COUNT
  • Train-Case: Words capitalized and hyphen-separated. Used in some CSS methodologies. Example: User-Profile-Card

Why Case Conversion Matters

Consistent naming conventions improve code readability and maintainability. When integrating with external APIs or databases, you often need to convert between conventions—for example, converting snake_case database columns to camelCase JavaScript properties, or converting PascalCase class names to kebab-case file names.

Language-Specific Conventions

  • JavaScript: camelCase for variables/functions, PascalCase for classes/components
  • Python: snake_case for functions/variables, PascalCase for classes
  • Go: PascalCase for exported names, camelCase for internal names
  • Rust: snake_case for functions/variables, PascalCase for types
  • CSS: kebab-case for class names and properties

Smart Detection

Our converter automatically detects the input case convention and converts to your selected output format. It handles edge cases like acronyms (URL, HTTP, ID) intelligently, ensuring getUserID converts to get_user_id rather than get_u_r_l.

Privacy First

All conversions happen locally in your browser. Your text is never sent to any server, making this tool safe for converting sensitive variable names or proprietary code identifiers.
tools.hash.seo.article:
What is a Hash Function?

A hash function is a mathematical algorithm that converts input data of any size into a fixed-size string of characters, typically a hexadecimal number. The same input always produces the same output, but even a tiny change in input produces a completely different hash. This property makes hash functions essential for data integrity verification, password storage, and digital signatures.

Supported Hash Algorithms

  • MD5 (128-bit): Once widely used, now considered cryptographically broken. Still useful for non-security purposes like checksums and cache keys. Not recommended for passwords or security applications.
  • SHA-1 (160-bit): Also deprecated for security uses due to collision vulnerabilities. Still found in legacy systems and Git (which uses it for content addressing, not security).
  • SHA-256 (256-bit): Part of the SHA-2 family, currently considered secure. Widely used in SSL certificates, cryptocurrency, and file integrity verification. Our recommended choice for most applications.
  • SHA-512 (512-bit): A longer variant of SHA-2, offering additional security margin. Used in high-security applications and systems processing large amounts of data.
  • HMAC: Hash-based Message Authentication Code combines a hash function with a secret key, providing both data integrity and authenticity verification. Essential for API authentication and webhook signatures.

Common Use Cases

  • File Integrity: Verify downloaded files match their expected checksums
  • Password Verification: Store password hashes instead of plaintext (use bcrypt/Argon2, not plain SHA)
  • API Signatures: Generate HMAC signatures for secure API requests
  • Data Deduplication: Identify duplicate files by comparing hashes
  • Git Commits: Every commit is identified by its SHA-1 hash

Privacy & Security

All hashing happens locally in your browser using the Web Crypto API. Your sensitive data never leaves your computer. This makes our tool safe for hashing passwords, API keys, or any confidential information.
tools.hash.s1.label: Understanding Hash Functions
tools.hash.s1.title: What Hash Functions Actually Do and Why You Keep Running Into Them
tools.hash.s1.body1: A hash function takes arbitrary input — a single character, a ten-gigabyte file, anything — and produces a fixed-length output called a digest or checksum. Feed it the same input twice and you get the same output, every single time. Change one bit of the input and the entire output changes unpredictably. The process is one-way: you cannot reconstruct the input from the output. Think of it as a digital fingerprint. Two different files can have the same fingerprint in theory, but a good hash function makes finding such a collision computationally infeasible. That is the entire point. If you have been writing software for any length of time, you have already used hash functions whether you realized it or not. Every git commit is identified by a SHA-1 hash of its contents. Password databases store hashes, not plaintext. When you download a Linux ISO, the publisher lists a SHA-256 checksum next to the download link so you can verify the file arrived intact. API providers sign requests with HMAC to prove authenticity. Deduplication systems in cloud storage use hashes to identify identical blocks without comparing data byte by byte. Package managers like npm and pip verify downloaded packages against published SHA-256 digests to guard against tampering. Even your browser uses hashes internally — TLS certificates are signed over a hash of the certificate contents, not the full certificate. The universality of hash functions is precisely what makes a dedicated hash generator useful. You do not always want to spin up a Python REPL or open a terminal just to compute SHA-256 of a string. Sometimes you need a quick checksum to paste into a config file, verify a webhook payload, or confirm that two files on disk are identical. That is the gap this tool fills.
tools.hash.s2.label: Legacy Algorithms
tools.hash.s2.title: MD5 and SHA-1: Still Around, Still Useful — But Not for Security
tools.hash.s2.body1: Ron Rivest designed MD5 in 1991 as a replacement for MD4. It produces a 128-bit digest, rendered as 32 hexadecimal characters. For over a decade it was the default hash function across the software industry — used in password storage, file integrity checks, digital signatures, and SSL certificates. Then, in 2004, Xiaoyun Wang's research group demonstrated practical collision attacks against MD5. They could generate two different inputs that produce the same MD5 digest. By 2008, researchers used this weakness to create a rogue CA certificate, proving that MD5 was no longer safe for any security-critical purpose. SHA-1 emerged as NIST's answer to MD5's weaknesses. It produces a 160-bit digest and held up longer, but its days were numbered too. In February 2017, Google and CWI Amsterdam published the SHAttered attack — the first practical collision against SHA-1. Producing that collision required roughly 9,223,372,036,854,775,808 SHA-1 computations, which cost about $110,000 in GPU time. That price tag means SHA-1 collisions are not cheap to produce, but they are well within the budget of nation-states and large organizations. Here is the nuance that gets lost in the "MD5 is broken" narrative: broken for collision resistance does not mean useless. MD5 and SHA-1 are still perfectly fine for non-adversarial use cases. If you want to verify that a file downloaded correctly over a trusted connection, MD5 will catch random corruption just fine. If you need a short, deterministic key for a cache entry, MD5 is fast and compact. Git still uses SHA-1 for content addressing, and Linus Torvalds has argued this is acceptable because git's merkle tree structure makes exploiting collisions extremely difficult in practice. The rule is straightforward: do not use MD5 or SHA-1 anywhere an attacker could benefit from a collision. That means no password hashing, no digital signatures, no certificate generation. But for checksums, deduplication keys, and quick content fingerprinting where security is not a concern, they remain practical choices.
tools.hash.s3.label: Modern Standards
tools.hash.s3.title: SHA-256 and SHA-512: The Workhorses of Modern Cryptography
tools.hash.s3.body1: The SHA-2 family, designed by the NSA and published by NIST in 2001, is the backbone of modern cryptographic hashing. SHA-256 and SHA-512 are the two most widely used variants, and as of 2026, no practical attacks exist against either. SHA-256 produces a 256-bit digest — 64 hex characters. It is the hash you encounter most often in daily development work. TLS certificates use it. Blockchain networks like Bitcoin build their entire architecture on double-SHA-256. Code signing certificates rely on it. Package managers verify downloads against SHA-256 checksums: when you run npm install and see a SHA-256 integrity check in the logs, that is this algorithm at work. Linux distribution package managers like apt and dnf do the same. SSH key fingerprints are SHA-256 hashes. Even the Subresource Integrity attributes you add to script tags use SHA-256. SHA-512 produces a 512-bit digest — 128 hex characters. It uses 64-bit arithmetic operations internally, which means on 64-bit hardware it can actually be faster than SHA-256 for large inputs despite doing more work. In practice, SHA-512 is overkill for most applications. SHA-256 already provides a security margin that would require breaking fundamental assumptions about mathematics to compromise. You would choose SHA-512 when working within a system that mandates it, or when you need the extra collision resistance for very large datasets. SHA-384 is also part of the SHA-2 family. It uses the same internal computation as SHA-512 but with different initial hash values and the final output truncated to 384 bits — 96 hex characters. You will primarily encounter SHA-384 in TLS configurations, where some cipher suites specify it for the PRF or for certificate signatures. If your compliance requirements or protocol specifications call for SHA-384, it provides the same security guarantees as SHA-512 with a slightly smaller output.
tools.hash.s4.label: SHA-224 and SHA-384
tools.hash.s4.title: SHA-224 and SHA-384: Niche but Necessary
tools.hash.s4.body1: SHA-224 and SHA-384 occupy a somewhat awkward position in the SHA-2 family — they are truncated versions of SHA-256 and SHA-512 respectively, and they exist primarily to satisfy specific standards and compliance requirements rather than filling a practical gap. SHA-224 uses the same internal logic as SHA-256 but starts with different initial hash values and truncates the output to 224 bits, producing a 56-character hex string. It was introduced to match the security level of 112-bit symmetric keys, which is a requirement in certain US government standards under FIPS 180-4. In everyday development you will rarely encounter SHA-224. It shows up occasionally in older government systems, some smart card protocols, and a handful of European digital signature standards that specified it before SHA-256 became ubiquitous. SHA-384, as mentioned earlier, truncates SHA-512 to 384 bits. It targets the 192-bit security level, which aligns with certain AES-192 configurations in government and military contexts. In the TLS ecosystem, SHA-384 appears in cipher suites that demand higher security assurance, and some certificate authorities use it for intermediate certificate signatures. The practical reality is that if you are reaching for SHA-224 or SHA-384, it is almost certainly because a specification, standard, or compliance framework told you to. Nobody picks these algorithms for a new greenfield project when SHA-256 and SHA-512 are available. But standards move slowly, legacy systems persist for decades, and interoperability demands that these hash functions remain available in any comprehensive tool.
tools.hash.s5.label: HMAC Explained
tools.hash.s5.title: HMAC: Why You Need a Secret Key, Not Just a Hash
tools.hash.s5.body1: A plain hash is a public operation. Anyone who knows the input can compute the same hash. That is by design — determinism is what makes hash functions useful for verification. But what happens when you need to prove not just that data has a certain fingerprint, but that the person producing that fingerprint knows a secret? That is exactly the problem HMAC solves. HMAC, or Hash-based Message Authentication Code, combines a hash function with a secret key to produce a keyed digest. The construction is defined in RFC 2104 and works with any underlying hash function — HMAC-SHA256, HMAC-SHA512, and so on. The critical distinction: hash("hello") produces the same output for everyone, but HMAC-SHA256("hello", "my-secret-key") can only be reproduced by someone who knows the key. This property makes HMAC indispensable across modern web infrastructure. When Stripe sends a webhook to your server, the request includes an HMAC-SHA256 signature computed over the request body using your webhook secret. Your server recomputes the HMAC with the same secret and compares it to the signature in the header. If they match, you know the request genuinely came from Stripe and was not tampered with. GitHub webhooks work the same way. AWS SigV4, the authentication protocol for every AWS API call, uses HMAC-SHA256 at multiple stages to sign requests. JWT tokens in their HS256 variant use HMAC-SHA256 for the signature portion. The pattern is consistent: the server shares a secret key with the client, both sides compute the HMAC over the message, and a match proves authenticity and integrity simultaneously. Without the key, an attacker cannot forge a valid signature even if they know exactly which hash algorithm is being used. This is why HMAC is fundamentally different from plain hashing — it provides authentication, not just integrity. When you are debugging webhook integrations or verifying API signatures, you need a tool that can compute HMAC digests, not just plain hashes.
tools.hash.s6.label: Practical Use Cases
tools.hash.s6.title: When You Actually Need to Compute a Hash in Practice
tools.hash.s6.body1: Most developers do not think about hash functions until a specific situation demands one. Here are the scenarios you are most likely to encounter. Verifying file downloads is the classic use case. When you download a Linux ISO, a firmware update, or a binary release from GitHub, the publisher typically lists a SHA-256 checksum alongside the download link. You compute the hash of the downloaded file and compare it to the published value. A match confirms the file is identical to what the publisher intended — no corruption during transfer, no man-in-the-middle tampering. Detecting duplicate files without comparing them byte by byte is another common scenario. If two files produce the same SHA-256 hash, they are almost certainly identical. Backup systems, photo management tools, and cloud storage platforms all use this approach for deduplication. Generating deterministic IDs from content comes up frequently in distributed systems. If you need a unique identifier for a piece of data and want it to be reproducible across different machines without coordination, hashing the content gives you exactly that. Git uses this pattern — every object is identified by the SHA-1 hash of its contents. Cache invalidation keys are often hashes of the cached content or query parameters. When the underlying data changes, the hash changes, and the cache naturally invalidates. This pattern appears in HTTP ETag headers, CDN cache keys, and build system artifact hashing. Data corruption detection in transit or at rest relies on hashes. Database systems compute checksums of pages or blocks to detect silent corruption from disk errors. Network protocols like TCP use checksums on every segment. All of these scenarios share a common thread: you need to compute a hash quickly, and you need the result to be reliable. This tool runs entirely in your browser using the Web Crypto API, which means your data never leaves your device. Whether you are hashing a password before storing it, verifying a download, debugging a webhook signature, or generating a content-based ID, the computation happens locally. No server-side processing, no network transmission, no logging. Your input stays on your machine.
tools.base64.seo.article:
What is Base64 Encoding?

Base64 is a binary-to-text encoding scheme that represents binary data using 64 ASCII characters (A-Z, a-z, 0-9, +, /). It's designed to safely transmit binary data through systems that only handle text, such as email, JSON, or XML. Every 3 bytes of binary data becomes 4 Base64 characters, resulting in approximately 33% size increase.

How Base64 Works

Base64 divides input data into 6-bit groups, each representing a value from 0 to 63. These values map to the Base64 alphabet. If the input isn't divisible by 3, padding characters (=) are added to the output. For example, the string "Hello" becomes "SGVsbG8=" in Base64.

Standard vs. URL-Safe Base64

  • Standard Base64: Uses + and / characters, which have special meanings in URLs and require percent-encoding
  • Base64URL: Replaces + with - and / with _, making it safe for URLs and filenames without additional encoding

Common Use Cases

  • Email Attachments: MIME encodes binary attachments in Base64
  • Data URIs: Embed small images directly in HTML/CSS using data:image/png;base64,...
  • JSON Web Tokens (JWT): JWTs use Base64URL encoding for header, payload, and signature
  • API Keys: Binary authentication tokens are often Base64-encoded for transmission
  • Certificate Encoding: SSL certificates in PEM format use Base64

Important: Encoding vs. Encryption

Base64 is not encryption. Anyone can decode Base64 without a key. Never use Base64 alone to protect sensitive data. For security, encrypt data first, then optionally Base64-encode the encrypted result for safe transmission.

Features of This Tool

Our Base64 encoder/decoder runs entirely in your browser. It supports standard and URL-safe variants, handles Unicode text correctly, and can encode/decode files up to 50MB. Your data is never uploaded to any server.
tools.url_encode.seo.article:
What is URL Encoding?

URL encoding (also called percent-encoding) is a mechanism for representing characters in a URL using only the ASCII character set. Characters outside this set, or those with special meaning in URLs, are converted to a % followed by two hexadecimal digits representing the character's byte value.

Why URL Encoding is Necessary

URLs have strict character limitations. Spaces, quotes, non-ASCII characters, and reserved characters like ?, &, = must be encoded when used as data rather than URL structure. Without encoding, these characters would break URL parsing or cause security vulnerabilities.

Encoding Methods

  • encodeURIComponent(): Encodes all special characters including /, ?, &. Use for individual URL components like query parameter values.
  • encodeURI(): Preserves URL structure characters (/, ?, &, =). Use for complete URLs.
  • RFC 3986: The strict standard that encodes additional characters like !, ', (, ), *

Common Encoding Examples

  • Space → %20 (or + in form data)
  • & → %26
  • = → %3D
  • Chinese characters like 你好 → %E4%BD%A0%E5%A5%BD
  • Emoji like 😀 → %F0%9F%98%80

When to Use Each Method

Use encodeURIComponent for query string values to prevent injection attacks. For example, if a search query contains &, encoding ensures it's treated as part of the search term, not a new parameter. Use encodeURI when you have a complete URL that needs encoding while preserving its structure.

Security Considerations

URL encoding prevents injection attacks and ensures URLs work across different systems. However, always validate and sanitize user input on the server side—encoding alone isn't sufficient for security. Our tool processes everything locally in your browser.
tools.word_counter.seo.article:
Why Word Count Matters

Word counting is essential for writers, students, marketers, and developers. Whether you're meeting academic requirements, optimizing content for SEO, or analyzing text complexity, accurate word counts help you communicate effectively and meet specifications.

Our Comprehensive Statistics

  • Word Count: Total words in your text, essential for article requirements
  • Character Count: Total characters including spaces (useful for character limits)
  • Characters (no spaces): Pure text length excluding whitespace
  • Sentence Count: Number of sentences, useful for readability analysis
  • Paragraph Count: Number of paragraphs for structure analysis
  • Unique Words: Vocabulary diversity indicator
  • Reading Time: Estimated time to read at average speed
  • Speaking Time: Estimated presentation duration

Understanding Readability Scores

The Flesch-Kincaid readability score ranges from 0-100, where higher scores indicate easier text:

  • 90-100: Very easy (5th grade level)
  • 80-90: Easy (6th grade level)
  • 70-80: Fairly easy (7th grade level)
  • 60-70: Standard (8th-9th grade level)
  • 50-60: Fairly difficult (10th-12th grade)
  • 30-50: Difficult (college level)
  • 0-30: Very difficult (graduate level)

Keyword Density for SEO

Keyword density shows how frequently words appear in your text. For SEO, target keywords typically should appear 1-2% of total word count. Higher densities may trigger spam filters. Our tool displays the top keywords and their density percentages.

Multilingual Support

Our word counter handles multiple languages correctly. Chinese, Japanese, and Korean characters are counted individually since these languages don't use spaces between words. Reading time estimates adjust based on language (Chinese averages 500 characters/minute vs. 238 words/minute for English).

Privacy First

All text analysis happens locally in your browser. Your documents are never transmitted to any server, making this tool safe for analyzing sensitive content, drafts, or confidential materials.

tools.ip.related.title: Related Developer Tools
tools.ip.guide.title: Deep Dive into IP Addresses
tools.ip.guide.text: Learn how IP addresses work, the difference between IPv4 and IPv6, and public vs private IP.
tools.ip.seo.article:
Understanding IP Addresses

An IP (Internet Protocol) address is a unique numerical identifier assigned to every device connected to a network using the Internet Protocol. It serves two main purposes: identifying the host or network interface, and providing location addressing for routing data across the internet.

IPv4 vs IPv6

  • IPv4: The original addressing system using 32-bit numbers (e.g., 192.168.1.1). Supports about 4.3 billion addresses, which has been exhausted due to internet growth. Still widely used, especially in local networks.
  • IPv6: The newer 128-bit addressing system (e.g., 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334). Provides virtually unlimited addresses (340 undecillion) to solve IPv4 exhaustion. Gradually being adopted worldwide.

Public vs Private IP Addresses

  • Public IP: Globally unique, assigned by your ISP, visible to the internet. Used for external communications and can be geolocated.
  • Private IP: Used within local networks (192.168.x.x, 10.x.x.x, 172.16-31.x.x). Not routable on the internet, reused across networks worldwide.

What IP Geolocation Reveals

IP geolocation can determine your approximate country, region, city, ISP, and timezone. However, it cannot pinpoint your exact physical address—typically accurate to city level at best. VPNs, proxies, and mobile networks can significantly reduce accuracy.

Understanding ASN

An Autonomous System Number (ASN) identifies a network block operated by a single organization (usually an ISP or large company). ASNs are used in BGP routing to direct traffic between major networks. Your ASN reveals which organization controls your IP address block.

Privacy Considerations

Your public IP is visible to every website you visit. Websites use it for geolocation, security (detecting suspicious activity), and content personalization. Using a VPN masks your real IP with the VPN server's address, providing privacy benefits.

Using This Tool

Our IP lookup tool shows your current public IP addresses (IPv4 and IPv6), and can look up geolocation data for any IP address. The interactive map shows approximate location. All lookups are performed server-side for accuracy.
tools.whois.seo.article:
What is Whois?

Whois is a query and response protocol used to query databases that store information about domain names, IP address blocks, and autonomous systems. When you register a domain, the registrar collects contact information and makes it available through Whois lookups.

Information Available in Whois

  • Registrar: The company where the domain was registered (e.g., GoDaddy, Namecheap)
  • Creation Date: When the domain was first registered
  • Expiration Date: When the registration expires (important for domain acquisition)
  • Updated Date: Last modification to the domain record
  • Status: Domain status codes (active, locked, pending transfer, etc.)
  • Name Servers: DNS servers handling the domain
  • Registrant Contact: Owner information (often redacted for privacy)

RDAP: The Modern Whois

RDAP (Registration Data Access Protocol) is the successor to Whois, returning structured JSON data instead of plain text. It provides better internationalization, authentication, and access control. Our tool uses RDAP when available and falls back to traditional Whois for compatibility.

Privacy Protection

Many domain owners use WHOIS privacy services (also called domain privacy or ID protection) to hide personal information. Instead of showing your name, email, and address, the public record shows the privacy service's contact details. This is especially important after GDPR made personal data protection mandatory.

Use Cases

  • Domain Research: Check availability, ownership history, and expiration dates
  • Competitive Analysis: Identify competitors' registrars and hosting
  • Security Investigations: Research suspicious domains for phishing or malware
  • Trademark Protection: Monitor domains similar to your brand
  • Technical Troubleshooting: Find contact information for domain issues

Looking Up IP Addresses

Whois also works for IP addresses, querying regional internet registries (ARIN, RIPE, APNIC, LACNIC, AFRINIC) to find who owns an IP block. This is useful for abuse reporting and network troubleshooting.

Limitations

Some information may be redacted due to privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA). Proxy services and privacy-protected domains hide registrant details. Our tool queries public databases and shows all available information.
tools.hash.s1.body2: The determinism of hash functions is what makes them useful. The same input always produces the same output, no matter who computes it or when. This property is what lets you verify a file download: the publisher computes the hash of the correct file and publishes it, you compute the hash of your downloaded copy, and if they match, you can be confident the file is intact. Change even a single bit in a multi-gigabyte file and the hash changes completely — a phenomenon called the avalanche effect. This sensitivity to input changes is what gives hash functions their verification power. The output looks random, but it is entirely deterministic: hash("hello") will always produce the same result on every compliant implementation.
tools.hash.s2.body2: The practical reality is that MD5 and SHA-1 are not going away anytime soon. Legacy systems, older protocols, and existing databases rely on them. If you are building something new, use SHA-256 or SHA-512. But if you are debugging a system that uses MD5 hashes for cache keys, or verifying a file checksum that was published as MD5, you still need to be able to compute these values. Understanding why they are broken for security purposes is important: collision resistance means two different inputs can produce the same output. For MD5, generating a collision takes less than a second on a modern laptop. For SHA-1, it requires significant computational resources but is still feasible for determined attackers with the right budget. Neither should be used for password hashing, digital signatures, or any scenario where an attacker might benefit from producing a collision.
tools.hash.s3.body2: The difference between SHA-256 and SHA-512 in practice comes down to performance on different architectures. On 64-bit processors, SHA-512 is actually faster because it processes data in 64-bit words. On 32-bit processors, SHA-256 has the edge. For most web applications, APIs, and general-purpose hashing, SHA-256 hits the sweet spot: widely supported, fast enough, and secure enough that nobody is even remotely close to breaking it. The Bitcoin network collectively computes quadrillions of SHA-256 hashes per second and has been doing so for over a decade without finding a collision. That is the level of computational effort required to break SHA-256 by brute force — effectively impossible with current and foreseeable technology.
tools.hash.s5.body2: A common mistake developers make with HMAC is using the hash output directly as a comparison token without constant-time comparison. If you compare HMACs using regular string equality, an attacker can exploit timing differences to gradually figure out the correct HMAC byte-by-byte. Always use a constant-time comparison function when verifying HMACs. Most modern frameworks provide this: Node.js has crypto.timingSafeEqual, Python has hmac.compare_digest, and Go has crypto/subtle.ConstantTimeCompare. Another consideration is key length: the HMAC specification handles keys shorter or longer than the block size by hashing them first, but using a key that matches the block size (64 bytes for SHA-256) is optimal.
tools.hash.s6.body2: One underappreciated use of hashing is content-addressed storage. Systems like IPFS, Git, and Docker all use hashes as identifiers for content. Instead of referring to a file by its name or location, you refer to it by its hash. This means the same content always has the same identifier, regardless of where it is stored or what it is named. Git uses SHA-1 (transitioning to SHA-256) for commit hashes. Docker image layers are identified by their SHA-256 digest. IPFS uses a multihash format that supports multiple hash algorithms. This approach enables deduplication (identical files stored once), integrity verification (tampering changes the hash), and distributed caching (any node can verify and serve content by its hash). Every time you run npm install, the package manager verifies SHA-512 integrity hashes for downloaded packages. Your browser verifies SHA-256 hashes of TLS certificates. Hash functions are woven into the infrastructure of the internet in ways most developers never think about directly.
